decks.name: Repeating Patterns
decks.description: Learn about patterns that repeat the same group of elements over and over

cards.back: A repeating pattern is a set of shapes, colours, or numbers that keeps coming back in the same order. For example: circle, square, circle, square, circle, square...
cards.hint: Think of a string of beads with the same colours coming again and again

cards.front: What is the 'core' of a repeating pattern?
cards.back: The core is the smallest group that repeats. In the pattern red, blue, red, blue, red, blue... the core is 'red, blue'. Everything else is just this core repeating.
cards.hint: Find the shortest piece that, when copied, makes the whole pattern

cards.front: In the pattern: triangle, triangle, circle, triangle, triangle, circle... what comes next?
cards.back: Triangle. The core is 'triangle, triangle, circle'. After the circle, the pattern starts again with triangle.
cards.hint: Find the group that keeps repeating

cards.front: Where do we see repeating patterns in real life?
cards.back: Repeating patterns are everywhere! Look at floor tiles, saree borders, rangoli designs, mehndi art, fence railings, and even the stripes on a zebra crossing.
cards.hint: Look at the borders on your clothes or the tiles in your kitchen

cards.front: Can sounds make a repeating pattern?
cards.back: Yes! Clap-clap-snap, clap-clap-snap is a repeating sound pattern. Music, dance steps, and even the beat of a dhol follow repeating patterns.
cards.hint: Think of the rhythm when you clap along to a song

decks.name: Growing Patterns
decks.description: Understand patterns where something increases or changes step by step

cards.back: A growing pattern is one where the elements increase or change in a regular way. For example: 1 dot, 3 dots, 5 dots, 7 dots... Each step adds 2 more dots.
cards.hint: In a growing pattern, each step is bigger or different from the last

cards.front: How is a growing pattern different from a repeating pattern?
cards.back: In a repeating pattern, the same group comes again and again. In a growing pattern, each step changes -- it usually gets bigger. For example, 2, 4, 6, 8... keeps growing by 2.
cards.hint: Repeating = same thing again; Growing = something new each time
Find the difference between each pair of numbers

cards.front: Can shapes make a growing pattern?
cards.back: Yes! Imagine building stairs with blocks: step 1 has 1 block, step 2 has 3 blocks, step 3 has 6 blocks. The shape grows bigger at each step.
cards.hint: Think of stacking blocks to make a staircase

cards.front: What is the rule for the pattern: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25?
cards.back: The rule is 'add 5 each time'. Each number is 5 more than the one before. This is also the table of 5!
cards.hint: Look at how much each number increases by

decks.name: Number Sequences & Pattern Rules
decks.description: Discover rules behind number patterns and predict what comes next

cards.back: A number sequence is a list of numbers that follow a certain rule. For example: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 is a sequence where you add 2 each time.
cards.hint: Think of numbers standing in a line, each connected by a rule

cards.front: How do you find the rule of a number pattern?
cards.back: Look at the difference between one number and the next. If the differences are the same, that is your rule. For example: 10, 15, 20, 25 -- the difference is always 5, so the rule is 'add 5'.
cards.hint: Subtract each number from the next one to find the difference
This is a decreasing pattern -- the numbers get smaller

cards.back: Odd numbers (1, 3, 5, 7, 9...) and even numbers (2, 4, 6, 8, 10...) each form a pattern. Odd numbers always end in 1, 3, 5, 7, or 9. Even numbers always end in 0, 2, 4, 6, or 8.
cards.hint: Check the last digit of the number

cards.front: Can patterns help us predict what comes next?
cards.back: Yes! Once you know the rule, you can find any number in the pattern. If the rule is 'add 3' and the pattern starts at 2: the next numbers are 5, 8, 11, 14, and so on.
cards.hint: Knowing the rule is like having a magic key to the whole pattern
